name: Euclid's Geometry — Axioms and Postulates
whatIsIt: Over 2000 years ago Euclid built all of geometry from a few simple, agreed-upon truths. AXIOMS are common truths used everywhere (like 'things equal to the same thing are equal to one another'). POSTULATES are the geometry-only starting rules (like 'a straight line can be drawn joining any two points'). From these, every other result is PROVED step by step as a theorem.
whyNeeded: This is where mathematics becomes a chain of reasons, not just facts to memorise. Understanding axioms and proof is the foundation for all of high-school geometry and for logical thinking in science, law and computing — you learn to justify every claim.
realLifeUses: Understanding why a proof is watertight, not just the answer
Logical reasoning used in debating, coding and law
Knowing a point, line and plane are ideal building blocks in design software
Seeing how big results are built from small agreed truths

vocabulary.meaning: A self-evident truth assumed without proof, used across all of mathematics (e.g. the whole is greater than a part).
A basic assumption specific to geometry (e.g. two points determine a unique line).
A statement PROVED true using axioms, postulates and earlier theorems.
A point has no length, breadth or thickness; a line has length only and extends endlessly.

story.title: Grandfather's Balance
story.text: Nanaji has two bags that each weigh the same as a 2 kg brick. 'Do the two bags weigh the same as each other?' he asks Ira. 'Of course — they both equal the brick!' Ira says. Nanaji nods: 'That is Euclid's very first axiom — things equal to the same thing are equal to one another. All of geometry is built from tiny obvious truths like this.'
story.extractedProblem: Bag A = 2 kg brick and Bag B = 2 kg brick. What can we say about Bag A and Bag B?
story.answerInStory: They are equal — things equal to the same thing are equal to one another (Euclid's first axiom).

shapes.label: two points determine exactly one straight line

visual.caption: Euclid's first postulate: through any two points there is exactly one straight line — a starting truth needing no proof.

teachingGallery.title: Truths we start from
teachingGallery.note: Axioms are general; postulates are about geometry; theorems are proved from them.

examples.caption: Postulate: two points fix a unique straight line.

props.label: the whole (5) is greater than a part (3)

examples.caption: Axiom: the whole is greater than any of its parts.
Euclid's famous 5th postulate leads to the idea of parallel lines.

standardMethod.summary: Tell axioms (general truths) from postulates (geometry rules), and know that theorems are proved from them. Use the axioms to justify equal-adding or equal-subtracting steps.
steps: Recognise an axiom: a truth used across all maths (equals added to equals stay equal; the whole exceeds a part).
Recognise a postulate: a geometry starting rule (two points → one line; all right angles are equal).
Understand a theorem must be PROVED from axioms and postulates, not just stated.
Apply axioms in reasoning: if a = b, then a + c = b + c (adding equals to equals).

workedExamples.problem: How many straight lines pass through two given distinct points?
steps: Two distinct points fix a line uniquely.

workedExamples.problem: If AB = PQ and PQ = XY, what can we say about AB and XY? (name the number of the axiom used: Euclid's 1st)
steps: Things equal to the same thing (PQ) are equal to one another.

commonMistakes.mistake: Mixing up axioms (general truths) with postulates (geometry-specific rules).
commonMistakes.fix: Axioms apply to all of maths (the whole > a part). Postulates are about geometry (two points make one line).

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking a theorem can be assumed true like an axiom.
commonMistakes.fix: A theorem must be PROVED from axioms, postulates and earlier theorems. Only axioms and postulates are assumed without proof.

easy.q: How many straight lines pass through two distinct points?
How many points are needed to fix a unique straight line?
hintLadder: One point lets many lines through.

challenge.q: If a = b and b = c, then a = c. How many quantities were shown equal to each other in the end (a, b, c)?
Ravi says a theorem is true without any proof, just like an axiom. What's the fix?

options.label: A theorem must be PROVED from axioms and postulates

challenge.answer: A theorem must be PROVED from axioms and postulates

options.label: All right angles are equal to one another
The whole is greater than the part

questions.answer: All right angles are equal to one another
hintLadder: Which one is only about geometry?

questions.q: Minimum points needed to fix a unique line?

teachBackPrompt: Tell a grown-up Euclid's first axiom using two bags that each equal the same brick, and explain the difference between an axiom (a general truth we assume), a postulate (a geometry starting rule), and a theorem (something we must prove).

revisionCard.summary: Euclid built geometry from AXIOMS (general truths: things equal to the same thing are equal; the whole > a part) and POSTULATES (geometry rules: two points → one line; all right angles equal). THEOREMS are proved from these. Only axioms and postulates are assumed without proof.
